skill.name: react-native-dev
skill.description: A practical React Native and Expo development guide for building production mobile apps. It covers setup, UI components, navigation, styling, animations, state, forms, networking, testing, performance, native capabilities, deployment, and CI/CD.
skill.summary: Build React Native and Expo apps with focused patterns for UI, navigation, state, testing, performance, and release work.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, environment, and heuristic issues, but most are markdown examples or inline code references in a React Native guide. I found no prompt injection attempt and no evidence of malicious exfiltration or executable skill code. One documentation example pipes a remote installer into Bash, so the skill should publish with a user-safety warning.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The testing guide recommends running a remote Maestro installer directly through Bash. This is a real user-safety risk because a compromised endpoint, network path, or changed installer could execute arbitrary commands on a user machine. The context is legitimate tool installation, so this is not evidence of malicious intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line clearly pipes a URL response into bash. The surrounding text shows a legitimate Maestro install workflow, reducing confidence that it is malicious but not reducing the command execution risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Spans Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are mostly inline code names, JSX examples, package install snippets, and markdown tables. They are reference content for React Native development, not Ruby backtick execution or hidden executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown text and fenced documentation examples. No runtime wrapper, script hook, or skill-side command execution mechanism is present in those lines.

low_findings.title: Environment and Secret Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The environment variable findings refer to examples that explain Expo public variables, server-only secrets, and placeholder keys. The content warns against exposing database credentials or write-capable API keys through public variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholders and explanatory comments rather than real credentials. The same section explicitly warns users not to expose sensitive credentials in public Expo variables.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Matches Are Relative Import Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem path traversal findings are examples of fragile relative imports and asset paths in documentation. They do not read files, write files, or construct paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences appear inside import and require examples. There is no filesystem API call or user-controlled path construction in the cited context.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Is Not Semantically Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined documentation examples for commands, network requests, environment variables, and paths into a critical heuristic. Manual review found a mobile development guide with no hidden prompt injection, no obfuscation, and no confirmed exfiltration flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged capabilities are expected subjects for a React Native guide. The reviewed context is educational documentation rather than executable behavior or malicious control flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Install Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The testing guide recommends running a remote Maestro installer directly through Bash. This is a real user-safety risk because a compromised endpoint, network path, or changed installer could execute arbitrary commands on a user machine. The context is legitimate tool installation, so this is not evidence of malicious intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line clearly pipes a URL response into bash. The surrounding text shows a legitimate Maestro install workflow, reducing confidence that it is malicious but not reducing the command execution risk.

content.user_title: Build React Native Apps with Expo Guidance
content.value_statement: React Native projects often need consistent decisions across navigation, state, networking, testing, and release workflows. This skill provides focused Expo and React Native guidance with practical patterns for production apps.
actual_capabilities: Guides new Expo project setup with Expo Router and common dependencies.
Recommends React Native UI components, image handling, safe areas, and platform controls.
Explains navigation patterns for stacks, tabs, links, modals, sheets, and context menus.
Provides patterns for forms, state management, networking, API routes, and webhooks.
Covers performance profiling, bundle analysis, testing, CI/CD, EAS build, and store release steps.
Documents native capabilities such as camera, location, permissions, haptics, notifications, and biometrics.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a runnable React Native project template.
It does not verify installed package versions or project-specific native configuration automatically.
Some commands require local review before execution, especially remote install commands.
Platform store rules and Expo SDK details can change after the skill content was written.

use_cases.title: Start an Expo App
use_cases.description: Create a new TypeScript Expo app with routing, safe areas, gestures, images, and recommended setup conventions.

use_cases.title: Improve App Quality
use_cases.description: Review navigation, state, forms, networking, performance, and test coverage before a production release.

use_cases.title: Plan Native Features
use_cases.description: Implement camera, location, notifications, biometrics, and permissions using Expo-compatible patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a New App Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a new Expo React Native app. Include project structure, routing, key packages, and first setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose UI Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my React Native screen requirements and recommend components, styling approach, safe area handling, and image strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Design Data Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help design state management, form validation, API calls, auth token handling, and offline behavior for this feature.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Expo app for performance, testing, CI/CD, EAS build, app store release, and SDK upgrade risks.

output_examples.input: Plan the foundation for a new Expo app with tabs and authentication.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering Expo Router layout, tab routes, auth flow boundaries, recommended packages, environment variables, and release preparation.

output_examples.input: Improve a slow product list screen in React Native.
output_examples.output: A performance review with list virtualization, memoization, image caching, render tracing, and bundle-size checks.

output_examples.input: Add camera uploads with permissions and tests.
output_examples.output: A feature plan covering permission prompts, Expo camera usage, upload flow, failure states, component tests, and E2E coverage.

best_practices: Review generated shell commands before running them, especially installers from remote URLs.
Keep public environment variables separate from server-only secrets and write-capable keys.
Profile on real devices before release and test critical login, checkout, and native-permission flows.
anti_patterns: Do not expose database credentials or private API keys through EXPO_PUBLIC variables.
Do not use FlatList or unoptimized images for large production lists without profiling.
Do not co-locate reusable components, utilities, or types inside Expo Router route folders.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance, patterns, checklists, and review support for React Native and Expo projects.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it cover Expo Router?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes stack, tab, modal, sheet, link, and route organization guidance for Expo Router.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Jest, React Native Testing Library, store tests, and E2E flows with Maestro.

faq.question: Are secrets handled safely?
faq.answer: The guidance distinguishes public Expo variables from server-only secrets, but users must still review their own configuration.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: One documentation example pipes a remote installer to Bash. Users should inspect installers before executing them.
